body_txt: This trope associates mustaches with comedies, comedians, entertainers, and fools. Mustaches have a complex history. They were a staple of the vaudeville genre and silent comedies because the mustache allowed actors to be more expressive with their faces during performances. Mustaches are also believed to make the wearer look friendly but professional at the same time . In comedies, mustaches were often worn by a Working-Class Hero and The Everyman , hardworking and oftentimes endearing characters whose heart is in the right place as they try to do the right thing. This is because mustaches are said to soften a person's face and give off a friendly demeanor. Not only that, but mustaches help the character convey their thoughts and emotions for non-verbal communication. In the vaudeville genre, mustaches help assist characters with exaggerated mouth movements. It can pair well with their body language, which can vary from cartoonish movements, emotions, mannerisms, and character ticks. In animation, big mustaches are looked upon favorably because they can cover up mouth movements and exaggerate their character's facial expressions, helping avoid the tedious and time-consuming task of lip-synching. Mustaches help with over-the-top facial expressions despite their normally serious demeanor and show how someone can appear bumbling and eccentric. Although there's no strict law on what mustaches are to be used for comedy, the toothbrush mustache and the Cantinflas mustache are the most famous examples. Because of the mustache's history with comedy, mustaches are often associated with foolish characters because the mustache implies the character's actions are supposed to be satirical and cartoonish. For foolish characters, a mustache indicates they are pretentious and not to be taken seriously. What they are saying is not to be taken seriously under any circumstances. They are idiots unworthy of respect because of how detached and out-of-touch they are with reality. After all, mustaches aren't as popular as they were in the 20th century. Depending on the style of the mustache, it is a sign that the wearer is not to be taken seriously and is a visual cue that a person is a subject of mockery or is mocking someone else. In rare instances, mustaches have been shown being worn by the Monster Clown or a Clown Species , but it's not a common practice. The mustaches would serve as a Shout-Out to the classic vaudevillian or an homage to silent comedies. There are 2 possible explanations for why mustaches aren't as common with depictions of clowns; firstly, mustaches were initially used to make a character look friendly and approachable, which undermines the fear factor of a scary clown. Secondly, mustachioed clowns aren't usually what comes to mind when it comes to images of coulrophobia, it's usually a clean-shaven man in red and white makeup . A mustache on a clown could indicate status and rank within their society and culture . It could also designate the benign or friendly clowns from the traditional evil clowns because the evil ones are clean-shaven. Can go with Fake Facial Hair , Good Hair, Evil Hair , Laughably Evil , and Dastardly Whiplash . Will overlap with Godwin's Law of Facial Hair and Charlie Chaplin Shout-Out with the toothbrush mustache. The eyebrow equivalent would be Big Ol' Eyebrows . Compare Mustache Vandalism .
